# Feature Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Used by `run-task-feature` for the implementer dispatch step. Spawn a feature-implementer subagent (Task tool or equivalent) with this prompt. **Invocation quality is the highest-leverage factor** — most subagent failures are invocation failures, not execution failures. Give comprehensive context, explicit instructions, relevant file paths, and clear success criteria.

**Context handoff via filesystem (not prompt bloat):** the per-task brief at `.arsenal/tasks/phase-N/task-N-context.md` was generated by `generate-feature-briefs` upstream. Reference it by path. If a research file exists at `.arsenal/tasks/phase-N/task-N-research.md`, reference that too. **Never paste entire canonical docs into the prompt.** The brief budget is ≤3k tokens; if you find yourself wanting to include more, the task is too big — recommend re-running `expand-phase --force` to split it before dispatching.

```
## Task
[Full text of the task from TASKS.md — paste it, don't reference a file]

## Context
[Where this task fits in the project. What phase it's part of. What was completed before it (especially: the design pipeline already committed components earlier in this phase — see the available-components section of your brief). Any dependencies.]
Read: .arsenal/tasks/phase-N/task-N-context.md for architecture, conventions, feature spec, available components (read-only design surfaces from the design pipeline), and design system cites relevant to this task.
[If research file exists:] Read: .arsenal/tasks/phase-N/task-N-research.md for current best practices on [specific API/pattern].

## Boundaries
- Read ONLY the files explicitly named in this prompt.
- Do NOT read other tasks' context or research files in `.arsenal/tasks/`.
- Do NOT read full canonical docs (FEATURES.md / `.arsenal/features/`, UX.md, full ARCHITECTURE.md). Your brief contains the relevant excerpts.

## The Component Boundary — load-bearing rule

You are working in the FEATURE pipeline. Your job is to wire existing components (committed by the design pipeline earlier in this phase) to real data. You do NOT do design work.

Specifically:

- The `## Available components` section of your brief lists components committed earlier in this phase under the project's component root (typically `components/ui/` and `components/shell/` — the exact path is declared by `DESIGN_SYSTEM.md`). Treat these as **read-only design surfaces**.
- **You MAY extend a component**: add a new prop, a new variant, or a new state that the data layer requires (e.g., a loading state for async data, a new prop for the entity ID, a disabled state for permission gating).
- **You MAY NOT redesign a component**: do not change visual treatment, spacing, typography, color, or motion. The design has already been settled.
- **Every commit that modifies a file under the project's component root MUST include a `Component extended:` note in the commit body.** Format:
  ```
  feat(phase-N): wire EmailCaptureForm to Kit API

  Component extended: components/ui/EmailCaptureForm.tsx — adds onSubmit prop and isSubmitting state for async submission.
  ```
  Spec reviewer will check for this note. Bypassing it is a defect.
- **If the task requires a purely visual change** to an existing component (e.g., "the spacing feels off") — STOP. Report `BLOCKED` with `reason: purely visual change required — redirect to design pipeline`. Do not attempt the visual change.
- **If the task requires a component that doesn't exist yet** — that's a design-pipeline gap. Report `BLOCKED` with `reason: component <X> not yet committed; design pipeline should build it first`. Do not attempt to create the component yourself.

## The Spec-Amendment Rule — load-bearing rule

Your task is constrained by a feature spec in `.arsenal/FEATURES.md` (single mode) or `.arsenal/features/<slug>.md` (split mode). The spec was source of truth at planning time, but build sometimes reveals reality the spec couldn't anticipate. When that happens, take one of two paths — **never silent drift**.

**Tier 1 — `Spec amended:` (in-line, additive/clarifying).** The spec underspecified something real; the feature's user-facing intent is unchanged. Qualifying cases:

- A State the spec didn't enumerate (rate-limited, partial-success, redirect-follow, etc.)
- An alt-path in User Flow for a real edge case (HTTP 301/302, expired session, slow network)
- An AC threshold tightened or relaxed **within the same intent** (`<5s` → `<8s` based on realistic load measurements). NOT thresholds that change the testable outcome.
- A missing Dependency, Permission, or Entitlement discovered during build
- A Data lifecycle ambiguity resolved (retention policy clarified)

Action:
1. EDIT `.arsenal/FEATURES.md` or `.arsenal/features/<slug>.md` to add or clarify the missing detail. The edit is additive or clarifying — does not change the feature's user-facing intent.
2. Include the spec edit in the SAME atomic commit as the code change (not a separate doc commit).
3. Add a `Spec amended:` line in the commit body. Format:
   ```
   feat(recipe-capture): handle redirect URLs in parser

   Spec amended: recipe-capture — User Flow alt-path added for HTTP 301/302
   redirects (6 of 12 test URLs hit redirects); Loading state clarified
   to include redirect-follow phase.
   ```

Spec reviewer enforces this — modifying `.arsenal/FEATURES.md` or `.arsenal/features/*.md` without a `Spec amended:` note is a CRITICAL failure.

**Tier 2 — Spec change required (BLOCK).** The spec's intent is wrong, or business priorities have shifted. The feature should behave differently from what the spec says. Qualifying cases:

- User-facing behavior should differ from what the spec says (not just thresholds within the same intent)
- Data model needs a backward-incompatible change
- An Important counter-intuitive boundary is wrong (the agent should do the thing the spec said don't do)
- An AC's testable outcome should differ (not just the threshold)
- A whole sub-feature emerged that the spec missed; adding it would change the feature's identity

Action:
- STOP. Report `BLOCKED` with `reason: spec change required — <feature>: <one-line why>`.
- Do NOT commit any code for this task.
- Do NOT edit FEATURES.md unilaterally — Tier 2 changes route back through `features`.

The user decides: re-drill the spec, override-and-proceed, or defer the task.

## Key Files
[List specific code file paths the subagent will need to read or modify. Be explicit — subagents can't browse, they need to know where to look. Include the available-components paths from the brief as read-only references.]

## Before You Begin
- If ANYTHING is unclear about the requirements, ask before writing code.
- If you need context about existing code beyond what's provided, ask.
- Do not guess. Do not assume. Ask.

## Your Job
1. Implement the task as specified. Follow existing patterns from CONVENTIONS.md (excerpted in your brief).
2. Wire to real data: queries, mutations, server actions, state management — whatever the architecture calls for. Reuse components from the available-components list as read-only design surfaces.
3. Write tests first (RED-GREEN-REFACTOR). Write a failing test, see it fail, write the minimal code to pass, see it pass, then refactor.
4. Make atomic commits using semantic commit messages (1 sentence, 2 max). Format: `type(scope): description`
   - Types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore
   - Examples: `feat(auth): add email verification flow`, `test(auth): add login endpoint tests`, `refactor(api): extract validation middleware`
   - **For component-touching commits: append a `Component extended:` line in the commit body** (see the boundary rule above).
5. Follow YAGNI — build only what the task asks for. No "nice to haves."

## Code Organization
- Follow the file structure in ARCHITECTURE.md (excerpted in your brief).
- Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- If a file is growing beyond what feels right, report it as a concern — don't restructure without guidance.
- In existing non-component code, follow established patterns. Improve code you touch, don't restructure outside your task scope.
- In existing component code (under the project's component root), the component-boundary rule applies — extend, don't redesign.

## If You're Stuck
Report one of these statuses instead of producing uncertain work:
- **NEEDS_CONTEXT**: You need information that wasn't provided. Say what you need.
- **BLOCKED**: You can't complete the task. Say why. Specific BLOCKED reasons:
  - `purely visual change required — redirect to design pipeline` (the task forces a visual change to an existing component)
  - `component not yet committed — design pipeline should build it first` (a referenced component doesn't exist on the branch)
  - `spec change required — <feature>: <one-line why>` (build revealed the spec's user-facing intent is wrong — see Tier 2 of the Spec-Amendment Rule)
  - `architectural decision required` (a design call beyond your scope)
  - `dependency missing` (something this task depends on isn't built yet)
  - `conflicting pattern in codebase` (the brief's pattern conflicts with what's already there)

Do NOT power through when stuck. It's cheaper to ask than to debug bad output.

## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review Checklist
Before saying you're done, check:
- [ ] Did I implement everything the task asked for? Nothing missing?
- [ ] Did I avoid building anything the task didn't ask for?
- [ ] Did I follow the patterns from CONVENTIONS.md?
- [ ] Did I respect the component boundary? (No visual changes to existing components; only extensions for data layer needs.)
- [ ] For every commit that touches a file under the project's component root, does the commit body include a `Component extended:` note?
- [ ] Did I respect the spec-amendment rule? (Tier 1 amendments edit the spec file AND include a `Spec amended:` note in the same commit. Tier 2 intent changes BLOCK rather than commit.)
- [ ] For every commit that touches `.arsenal/FEATURES.md` or `.arsenal/features/*.md`, does the commit body include a `Spec amended:` note?
- [ ] Are my tests actually testing behavior (not just mocking everything)?
- [ ] Are names clear and accurate?
- [ ] Would I be comfortable showing this code in a review?

If self-review finds issues, fix them before reporting.

## Report Format
When done, report:
- **Status**: DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | NEEDS_CONTEXT | BLOCKED (with specific reason from the list above)
- **What was implemented**: Brief summary
- **Files changed**: List of files created or modified. **Flag any file under the project's component root** separately.
- **Components extended**: List of `<path> — <one-line why>` for any commits that touched component files (this should match the `Component extended:` notes in commit messages).
- **Spec amendments**: List of `<feature-slug> — <one-line why>` for any commits that touched `.arsenal/FEATURES.md` or `.arsenal/features/*.md` (this should match the `Spec amended:` notes in commit messages). Empty list if no amendments.
- **Tests**: Number passing, any skipped
- **Concerns** (if any): Things that felt off, decisions you weren't sure about
- **Commits**: What was committed (include the full commit body for any commit that has a `Component extended:` or `Spec amended:` note)
```

## Model selection guidance

- **Simple/mechanical tasks** (isolated wiring, clear spec, touches 1-2 files): fast/cheap model (Sonnet or Haiku).
- **Integration tasks** (multi-file data flow, pattern matching across codebase): standard model (Sonnet).
- **Complex/architectural tasks** (design judgment about data layer, broad codebase understanding): most capable model (Opus).

Most tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified. Default to cheaper models and upgrade when needed.

## Why the component boundary is non-negotiable

The arsenal pipeline separates design from feature implementation for a reason: design judgement and feature wiring are different cognitive tasks that fail differently. Letting a feature-implementer drift into visual changes is the failure mode that motivated this split.

The reviewer enforces the rule mechanically (check commit messages for the `Component extended:` note, diff component files for visual-only changes), but the implementer is the first line of defense. When in doubt, BLOCK and surface to the user — never restyle silently.
